narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in higher education, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admissions model looks at patterns from thousands of past students to predict whether someone is likely to pass the bar exam on their first try, and unfortunately, it estimated an 88.3% chance that you would not pass, which is why your application was denied—schools use this to prioritize applicants predicted to succeed right away. Your profile includes being in the lowest decile for grades in both your first and third years of school, an undergraduate GPA of 2.90, an LSAT score of 29, middle family income, and planning to attend full-time, which is the most common choice among students. What tipped the scales most strongly toward this failure prediction were your low year-three school decile and low year-one decile, as these stood out as the biggest factors pushing the model to see higher risk compared to typical students who rank much higher in those deciles—around the middle of their classes. Your undergraduate GPA actually nudged things slightly in a positive direction toward passing, better aligning with averages that support success, while your LSAT score had a small pull toward failure since it's below the usual 36.7 benchmark. Family income and full-time status played very minor roles, with income offering a tiny positive lean and full-time being neutral as it's so common. Overall, your academic deciles were far below the dataset averages of about 5.5 to 5.7, making your situation look riskier than most applicants who had stronger early and later school performances. This doesn't reflect your full potential or resilience as a Black woman navigating these paths, but it's how the model weighed the data. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to average, strengthening your recent grades if possible through additional coursework, or exploring programs with holistic reviews that value your unique background. You might also seek mentorship from successful Black lawyers who can guide targeted improvements. Keep pushing—many have overcome similar predictions to pass the bar and thrive. We're here to support your next steps.
